comments.body: This will be downvoted. What did you try so far?
What if firstname or lastname have a comma?
write java code to open the file, read line by line, split the line and store relevant data into Object.

answers.body:
Here's all the pieces you should need to put together a solution.

To read a file line by line use a BufferedReader:

BufferedReader reader = new BufferedReader( new FileReader( filename ) );

Now you can read a line by calling, for example 

String line = reader.readLine();

To split the line into its comma separated elements you could use String.split. eg. 

String[] token = line.split( "," );

You should be able to put together a solution using these tools and some creativity :)

comments.body: Thank you pandaadb, but unfortunately I have no control of this file. So I can't reformat it.

In that case Chronos answer would be fine for you. In terms of your objects, I think it is good practice to separate Book and Author. Book can then contain a list of authors for you. Or an author can contain a list of books or both (this depends on what your application wants to do).

There is only one type of object in the file: books with a title, year, ISBN, and multiple author names. In AWK terms, the RS (record separator) is a blank line. It is perfectly straightforward to parse.

answers.body:
Is your "formatted" file really formatted? You will run into problems because there are two types of objects in your file. And they are not recognizable from a parser perspective. This means that you will need custom logic where you analyze what you parse, and then need to decide what object it is and how to store it.

One approach for this could be http://opencsv.sourceforge.net/ Your file looks like a csv file to me and could be parsed as such.

If you have control of your formatted data, you should change it to make it clearer what the purpose it is. You'd have a few options, for example:

  1. Separate into 2 CSV files You could have 1 file for books, another for authors. The opencsv lib above will then automatically parse your files into Java objects for you that you can use.

  2. Create a json format for your file

for example, instead of formatting it like above, you can use:

{
 "title" : "mytitle",
 "year": 2001,
 "isbn" : 1231312,
 "authors" : [ "author1", "author2" ]
} ...

This format will enable you to use GSON or Jackson object mappers to parse those files, at which point it becomes very easy. Google will have plenty of examples on how to use that.

Hope that helps,

artur

Input is formatted

The accepted Answer claims your file is not really formatted. Not so.

Your entities are separated by a blank line. That makes parsing a simple matter.

This may not be the best formatting for hierarchical data (book + list of authors). But it does work. You should suggest to the publisher of this data that they switch to a standard format intended for hierarchical data. Examples include XML and JSON.

But read on for current solution.

I will assume for simplicity that your files are small enough for their content the be copied entirely into memory.

First write a sample file. I used same as your example, but put in some author names for easier reading.

String content = """
        title1, 2010, isbn, 121212
        author1, Alice, Anderson
        author2, Bob, Barker
        
        title2, 1999, isbn, 565656
        author1, Alice, Anderson
        
        title3, 2001, isbn, 898989
        author1, Alice, Anderson
        author2, Bob, Barker
        author3, Carol, Conway
        """;
try
{
    Files.writeString( path , content ); // Writes UTF-8.
    IO.println( "INFO String written to file: " + path.toAbsolutePath() );
}
catch ( IOException e )
{
    System.err.println( "ERROR Error writing to file: " + e.getMessage() );
    e.printStackTrace();
}

Define a class to represent our entity Book, and sub-entity Author.

If the main purpose of our class is to transparently communicate shallowly-immutable data, define the class as a record. Each Book object carries a sequenced collection of Author objects. They are sequenced because usually a primary author's name appears first.

record Author( String id , String firstName , String lastName ) { }
record Book( String title , Year year , String isbn , SequencedCollection < Author > authors ) { }

Read the data file. First split on the blank line of text (/n/n) to get an array of strings, each string representing one of our entities (book with authors). For each of those entity strings, read the first line for the book fields. Then read successive lines for the authors.

        System.out.println( "INFO Reading lines from file." );

        @Nullable String input = null;
        try
        {
            input = Files.readString( path , StandardCharsets.UTF_8 );
        }
        catch ( IOException e )
        {
            System.err.println( "ERROR Error reading file: " + e.getMessage() );
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
        Objects.requireNonNull( input );

        String[] entities = input.split( "\n\n" );
        for ( String entity : entities )
        {
            String[] lines = entity.split( "\n" );

            // First line is the book info.
            String[] bookFields = lines[ 0 ].split( ", " ); // Delimiter = COMMA + SPACE.

            String title = bookFields[ 0 ];
            if ( title.isBlank() ) throw new IllegalStateException();

            Year year = Year.parse( bookFields[ 1 ] ); // Throws DateTimeParseException for invalid input.

            String ignore = bookFields[ 2 ];
            if ( ! ignore.equals( "isbn" ) ) throw new IllegalStateException();

            String isbn = bookFields[ 3 ];
            if ( isbn.isBlank() ) throw new IllegalStateException();

            // Further lines are the authors.
            SequencedCollection < Author > authors = new ArrayList <>() ;
            for ( int index = 1 ; index < lines.length ; index++ )  // Annoying zero-based index counting. So `1` means two.
            {
                String line = lines[ index ];
                if ( line.isBlank() ) throw new IllegalStateException();
                String[] authorFields = line.split( "," );
                if ( authorFields.length != 3 ) throw new IllegalStateException();
                Author author = new Author( authorFields[ 0 ] , authorFields[ 1 ] , authorFields[ 2 ] );
                authors.add( author );
            }

            // Final assembly.
            Book book = new Book( title , year , isbn , List.copyOf( authors ) ); // Use unmodifiable list for authors. 
            IO.println( book );
        }

When run:

INFO String written to file: /Users/basil_dot_work/bogus_books.txt
INFO Reading lines from file.
Book[title=title1, year=2010, isbn=121212, authors=[Author[id=author1, firstName= Alice, lastName= Anderson], Author[id=author2, firstName= Bob, lastName= Barker]]]
Book[title=title2, year=1999, isbn=565656, authors=[Author[id=author1, firstName= Alice, lastName= Anderson]]]
Book[title=title3, year=2001, isbn=898989, authors=[Author[id=author1, firstName= Alice, lastName= Anderson], Author[id=author2, firstName= Bob, lastName= Barker], Author[id=author3, firstName= Carol, lastName= Conway]]]

Here is complete code for your copy-paste convenience.

package work.basil.example.csv;

import org.jspecify.annotations.NullMarked;
import org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable;

import java.io.IOException;
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;
import java.nio.file.Files;
import java.nio.file.Path;
import java.nio.file.Paths;
import java.time.Year;
import java.util.*;

@NullMarked
public class ExParseGroupedData
{
    private void writeFile ( final Path path )
    {
String content = """
        title1, 2010, isbn, 121212
        author1, Alice, Anderson
        author2, Bob, Barker
        
        title2, 1999, isbn, 565656
        author1, Alice, Anderson
        
        title3, 2001, isbn, 898989
        author1, Alice, Anderson
        author2, Bob, Barker
        author3, Carol, Conway
        """;
try
{
    Files.writeString( path , content ); // Writes UTF-8.
    IO.println( "INFO String written to file: " + path.toAbsolutePath() );
}
catch ( IOException e )
{
    System.err.println( "ERROR Error writing to file: " + e.getMessage() );
    e.printStackTrace();
}
    }

    private void readFile ( final Path path )
    {
        System.out.println( "INFO Reading lines from file." );

        @Nullable String input = null;
        try
        {
            input = Files.readString( path , StandardCharsets.UTF_8 );
        }
        catch ( IOException e )
        {
            System.err.println( "ERROR Error reading file: " + e.getMessage() );
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
        Objects.requireNonNull( input );

        String[] entities = input.split( "\n\n" );
        for ( String entity : entities )
        {
            String[] lines = entity.split( "\n" );

            // First line is the book info.
            String[] bookFields = lines[ 0 ].split( ", " ); // Delimiter = COMMA + SPACE.

            String title = bookFields[ 0 ];
            if ( title.isBlank() ) throw new IllegalStateException();

            Year year = Year.parse( bookFields[ 1 ] ); // Throws DateTimeParseException for invalid input.

            String ignore = bookFields[ 2 ];
            if ( ! ignore.equals( "isbn" ) ) throw new IllegalStateException();

            String isbn = bookFields[ 3 ];
            if ( isbn.isBlank() ) throw new IllegalStateException();

            // Further lines are the authors.
            SequencedCollection < Author > authors = new ArrayList <>() ;
            for ( int index = 1 ; index < lines.length ; index++ )  // Annoying zero-based index counting. So `1` means two.
            {
                String line = lines[ index ];
                if ( line.isBlank() ) throw new IllegalStateException();
                String[] authorFields = line.split( "," );
                if ( authorFields.length != 3 ) throw new IllegalStateException();
                Author author = new Author( authorFields[ 0 ] , authorFields[ 1 ] , authorFields[ 2 ] );
                authors.add( author );
            }

            // Final assembly.
            Book book = new Book( title , year , isbn , List.copyOf( authors ) ); // Use unmodifiable list for authors.
            IO.println( book );
        }
    }

    record Author( String id , String firstName , String lastName ) { }
    record Book( String title , Year year , String isbn , SequencedCollection < Author > authors ) { }

    static void main ( )
    {
        Path path = Paths.get( System.getProperty( "user.home" ) , "bogus_books.txt" );
        ExParseGroupedData app = new ExParseGroupedData();
        app.writeFile( path );
        app.readFile( path );
    }
}

By the way, ASCII and Unicode offer characters to use as delimiters for fields, records, and groups. These could be used instead of COMMA and LINE FEED.

Separator Type Code Point Decimal Official Unicode Name Alternate Name( s )
File Separator U+001C 28 INFORMATION SEPARATOR FOUR file separator (FS)
Group Separator U+001D 29 INFORMATION SEPARATOR THREE group separator (GS)
Record Separator U+001E 30 INFORMATION SEPARATOR TWO record separator (RS)
Unit (Field) Separator U+001F 31 INFORMATION SEPARATOR ONE unit separator (US)

In real work, I would want a type for ISBN rather than using naked strings. I would look for a library that defines a class for the purpose of handling valid ISBN numbers, like the library fast-isbn-validation-java.

title: Loading formatted data from text file in an object
body:
I would like to read a text file with the following format:

title1, 2010, isbn, 121212
author1, firstname, lastname
author2, firstname, lastname

title2, 1999, isbn, 565656
author1, firstname, lastname

title3, 2001, isbn, 898989
author1, firstname, lastname
author2, firstname, lastname
author3, firstname, lastname

What is the best way to load each data in object(s) using an object-oriented programming language like Java ?

Do I have to create one class where the attributes are title, year, isbn and authors like this:

public class Book {

    private String title;
    private String year;
    private String isbn;
    private List<String> author; // A list because I don't know how many authors wrote the book
}

or multiples classes, one for each entity:

public class Book {

    private String title;
    private String year;
    private String isbn;
}

public class Author {

    private String firstname;
    private String lastname;
}

Thanks.
